skill.name: sca-blackduck
skill.description: Guides Software Composition Analysis with Synopsys Black Duck. It helps teams scan dependencies, review CVEs, assess license risk, generate SBOMs, and add CI/CD policy gates.
skill.summary: Run Black Duck SCA workflows for dependency, license, SBOM, and supply chain risk review.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many severe-looking patterns, but most are documentation, policy terminology, or intentionally vulnerable examples for SCA education. The real concern is copyable CI guidance that uses network shell installers and handles Black Duck or GitHub secrets. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found, so publication is acceptable with a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Network Script Piped to Shell in Copyable Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill and CI templates include ready-to-run commands that download scripts over HTTPS and execute them with bash. This is a real supply chain risk if users adopt the snippets without pinning or verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are executable examples that fetch remote scripts and immediately run them. The intent is legitimate Black Duck or tfsec setup, but the execution pattern is inherently risky.

medium_findings.title: CI Templates Handle High-Value Secrets
medium_findings.description: The workflow examples pass GitHub and Black Duck tokens into scanner actions and pipeline environments. This is expected for SCA, but copied templates need strict secret storage and log hygiene.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations clearly reference CI secrets and Black Duck credentials. They use standard secret mechanisms, so the concern is operational exposure rather than malicious collection.

medium_findings.title: Referenced Helper Scripts Are Not Present
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users to run helper scripts, but the scanned file tree does not include a scripts directory. Users may follow incomplete guidance or create unreviewed substitutes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation cites scripts that are absent from the listed skill files. This is not malicious, but it weakens reproducibility and may lead to unsafe user-created replacements.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Detections Are Scan Exclusions
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged hidden paths, but the cited lines are exclusion patterns for dependency scan configuration. They do not read hidden files or collect private data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths appear under scanner exclusion lists. They reduce scan scope and are not filesystem access instructions.

low_findings.title: Security Policy Terms Trigger Weak Crypto and Blocker Rules
low_findings.description: Many high-severity static matches are ordinary security policy words such as critical, high, blocklist, and compliance framework names. The context is policy configuration, not cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled lines are policy metadata and vulnerability triage references. I did not find evidence that they implement weak cryptography or offensive tooling.

low_findings.title: Intentionally Vulnerable Examples in Reference Material
low_findings.description: Reference files contain examples of HTML injection, credential exfiltration, command execution, and backdoor patterns. They are clearly presented as unsafe examples for threat education, not as active skill behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous snippets are labeled as vulnerable or malicious examples. They still warrant caution because marketplace users may copy reference material.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Script Piped to Shell in Copyable Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill and CI templates include ready-to-run commands that download scripts over HTTPS and execute them with bash. This is a real supply chain risk if users adopt the snippets without pinning or verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are executable examples that fetch remote scripts and immediately run them. The intent is legitimate Black Duck or tfsec setup, but the execution pattern is inherently risky.

dangerous_patterns.title: Intentionally Vulnerable Examples in Reference Material
dangerous_patterns.description: Reference files contain examples of HTML injection, credential exfiltration, command execution, and backdoor patterns. They are clearly presented as unsafe examples for threat education, not as active skill behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous snippets are labeled as vulnerable or malicious examples. They still warrant caution because marketplace users may copy reference material.

content.value_statement: Open source dependencies can introduce vulnerabilities, license obligations, and supply chain risk. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code run Black Duck-centered SCA workflows with practical remediation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains Black Duck SCA workflows for vulnerability, license, and component risk review.
Provides CI/CD templates for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins integration.
Maps dependency findings to CVE, CWE, OWASP, and compliance contexts.
Guides SBOM generation and dependency policy enforcement.
Offers remediation strategies for vulnerable, outdated, or risky components.
limitations: Requires access to a configured Black Duck instance and valid API credentials.
Does not include executable scripts referenced by the documentation.
Cannot verify vulnerability exploitability without project context and scan results.
License decisions still require legal or compliance review.

use_cases.title: Review dependency risk before release
use_cases.description: Scan a project, triage critical CVEs, and prepare remediation priorities for release approval.

use_cases.title: Add SCA gates to CI/CD
use_cases.description: Adapt the bundled CI templates to fail builds on high-risk dependency policy violations.

use_cases.title: Assess license and SBOM readiness
use_cases.description: Review component licenses, identify policy exceptions, and generate SBOM outputs for audits.

prompt_templates.title: Scan a project baseline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Black Duck SCA workflow to plan a baseline dependency scan for this repository. Identify manifests and required credentials.

prompt_templates.title: Triage scan findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these Black Duck findings. Prioritize critical and high CVEs, map them to CWE or OWASP categories, and recommend fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a CI gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Black Duck CI/CD integration plan for this project. Include secret handling, policy thresholds, artifacts, and failure behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Build a supply chain risk review
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a supply chain risk assessment using Black Duck results, license policy, SBOM outputs, and dependency health signals.

output_examples.input: Review a Black Duck scan with several critical dependency findings.
output_examples.output: A prioritized remediation report with affected components, severity, business impact, fixed versions, owners, and target dates.

output_examples.input: Add Black Duck checks to a pull request workflow.
output_examples.output: A CI integration plan covering credentials, policy gates, scan artifacts, SBOM output, and pull request feedback.

output_examples.input: Assess license compliance for production dependencies.
output_examples.output: A license risk summary with high-risk components, recommended replacements, policy exceptions, and legal review items.

best_practices: Store Black Duck tokens in approved CI secrets or a secrets manager.
Pin and verify downloaded scanner tools before use in production pipelines.
Document accepted risk with owners, expiration dates, and compensating controls.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API tokens into prompts, logs, or repository files.
Do not run network installer commands without reviewing the downloaded script source.
Do not suppress dependency findings without a tracked business justification.

faq.question: Does this skill run Black Duck by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows, templates, and guidance. Users need Black Duck access and local or CI execution.

faq.question: Which package managers are covered?
faq.answer: The guidance covers common ecosystems including npm, pip, Maven, Gradle, NuGet, Go modules, Composer, Cargo, and containers.

faq.question: Can it help with license compliance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes license risk guidance and policy templates, but final decisions need legal or compliance review.

faq.question: Can it generate an SBOM?
faq.answer: It explains SBOM workflows for Black Duck outputs, including CycloneDX and SPDX-oriented use cases.

faq.question: Is it safe for production CI pipelines?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review installer commands, pin trusted tool versions, and protect secrets before adopting the templates.

faq.question: Does it replace vulnerability management?
faq.answer: No. It supports triage and reporting, but teams still need ownership, SLAs, tracking, and risk acceptance processes.
